title: Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0 — Part 2: Student AI Competency Framework
sourceDocument: AI Competency Framework — For Eduversal Partner Schools (v1.0 PDF)
sourcePart: Part Two: Student AI Competency Framework

_meta.purpose: Verbatim canonical content for Part 2 of the Eduversal AI Competency Framework v1.0. Source of truth for student AI literacy learning outcomes across age bands. Chip refIds follow pattern student.{ageBand}.{strand} — e.g. student.primary.strand1.
_meta.writingNote: Verbatim from PDF. Paragraphs preserved as array entries.

verbatim: The Student AI Competency Framework establishes age-appropriate learning outcomes for AI literacy across year groups. It ensures students develop systematic understanding of AI and skills for responsible use as they progress through school.
AI literacy is not a separate subject but should be integrated across the curriculum. Subject teachers share responsibility for developing student AI competency through their teaching.

competencyStrands._overview: Student AI competency is organised into four strands.

strands.title: Understanding AI
strands.scope: Knowledge of what AI is, how it works, and its capabilities and limitations.

strands.title: Using AI Responsibly
strands.scope: Skills for using AI tools effectively, ethically, and with appropriate critical evaluation.

strands.title: Evaluating AI Outputs
strands.scope: Ability to assess AI-generated content for accuracy, bias, quality, and appropriateness.

strands.title: AI and Society
strands.scope: Understanding of AI's broader impacts on society, work, and human life.

primary.summary: At Primary level, students develop foundational awareness of AI as a type of technology, beginning to understand that computers can be programmed to perform tasks that seem intelligent. Direct use of generative AI tools is limited and closely supervised; the focus is on conceptual understanding and foundational digital literacy.

strand1.title: Understanding AI — Primary
verbatim: By the end of Primary, students can explain that some computer programs can learn from examples and data, which is called artificial intelligence. They can identify examples of AI in everyday life, such as voice assistants, recommendation systems, and smart home devices.
Students understand that AI is created by people and programmed to do specific tasks. They can explain that AI follows patterns it has learned but does not think or understand like humans do.

Students can give examples of things AI can do well (recognising images, translating languages, answering questions) and things AI cannot do (truly understand meaning, have feelings, be creative in the human sense).

strand2.title: Using AI Responsibly — Primary
verbatim: By the end of Primary, students understand that they should ask a teacher or parent before using AI tools. They know that information from AI should be checked because it might be wrong.
Students can follow simple guidelines for appropriate AI use in school contexts. They understand that using AI to do their work for them is not honest and does not help them learn.
Students treat AI tools with appropriate expectations, understanding they are helpful tools but not friends, teachers, or authority figures.

strand3.title: Evaluating AI Outputs — Primary
verbatim: By the end of Primary, students understand that AI can make mistakes, even when it sounds confident. They can identify obviously incorrect information in AI outputs with teacher guidance.
Students know to check AI information against other sources like books, teachers, and trusted websites. They can ask questions like 'Does this make sense?' and 'How can I check if this is true?'

strand4.title: AI and Society — Primary
verbatim: By the end of Primary, students can discuss ways AI helps people in daily life, such as accessibility tools for people with disabilities, safety features in cars, and medical diagnosis support.

Students understand that people have different views about AI and that it is important to think carefully about how we use technology. They can discuss simple questions about fairness and kindness related to technology use.

AI concepts at Primary level should be integrated naturally into existing curriculum areas. Computing and digital literacy lessons can introduce basic AI concepts. Science lessons can explore how AI learns from data and patterns. English and language lessons can discuss AI-generated stories and the value of human creativity. PSHE and character education can address responsible technology use and digital citizenship.
Direct use of generative AI tools should be limited to teacher-led demonstrations and carefully supervised activities with clear learning purposes.

lowerSecondary.summary: At Lower Secondary level, students develop more sophisticated understanding of AI and begin guided use of AI tools with explicit instruction on responsible practices. They learn to critically evaluate AI outputs and consider ethical implications of AI use.

strand1.title: Understanding AI — Lower Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Lower Secondary, students can explain how machine learning works at a conceptual level, understanding that AI systems learn patterns from large amounts of training data. They can distinguish between different types of AI, including generative AI, analytical AI, and predictive AI.

Students understand key concepts including training data and how it shapes AI behaviour, algorithms and how they process information, and the difference between narrow AI (designed for specific tasks) and general intelligence.

Students can explain important limitations of AI, including that AI can produce false information confidently (hallucination), AI may reflect biases present in training data, AI lacks genuine understanding, reasoning, and common sense, and AI cannot access information beyond its training without special tools.
Students understand that AI development is ongoing and capabilities are changing rapidly.

strand2.title: Using AI Responsibly — Lower Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Lower Secondary, students can use approved AI tools for appropriate learning tasks with teacher guidance. They understand and follow school policies on AI use, including what is permitted and prohibited.
Students can write clear prompts to get useful AI outputs and can refine prompts to improve results. They know to verify AI-generated information before using it.

Students understand academic integrity requirements related to AI, including when AI use must be declared and when it is not permitted. They can explain why submitting AI-generated work as their own is dishonest.
Students protect their privacy when using AI tools, understanding what information is appropriate to share.

strand3.title: Evaluating AI Outputs — Lower Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Lower Secondary, students consistently verify AI-generated information against credible sources. They can identify common AI errors including factual mistakes, false citations, anachronisms, and logical inconsistencies.

Students can recognise when AI outputs are generic, superficial, or missing important nuance. They can identify potential bias in AI outputs, including cultural assumptions, stereotypes, and one-sided perspectives.
Students can evaluate whether AI output is appropriate and useful for a specific purpose or task. They understand that AI confidence does not indicate accuracy.

strand4.title: AI and Society — Lower Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Lower Secondary, students can discuss benefits and risks of AI for society, including impacts on employment, privacy, information quality, and human relationships.

Students understand that AI systems can perpetuate or amplify social biases if not carefully designed and monitored. They can discuss issues of fairness and equity related to AI, including who benefits and who might be disadvantaged.

Students can consider multiple perspectives on AI-related issues, understanding that reasonable people disagree about appropriate AI use and development. They understand the importance of human oversight and decision-making in AI systems.
Students can discuss their own responsibilities as AI users, including honesty, respect for others, and thoughtful engagement.

AI literacy at Lower Secondary should be integrated across subjects while also receiving explicit attention in computing and digital literacy curriculum. Computing lessons can explore how AI systems work in more technical depth. English and humanities lessons can examine AI-generated content critically and discuss societal implications. Science and mathematics lessons can explore AI applications in relevant fields. All subjects can address responsible AI use for learning tasks within their domain.
Students should have opportunities for guided AI use with clear learning purposes and explicit instruction on responsible practices.

upperSecondary.summary: At Upper Secondary level, students develop sophisticated AI literacy preparing them for higher education and careers in an AI-transformed world. They use AI as a productivity and learning tool while maintaining academic integrity and critical perspective.

strand1.title: Understanding AI — Upper Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Upper Secondary, students can explain how large language models and other AI systems work at a more detailed level, including concepts of neural networks, parameters, and training processes.

Students understand technical factors that influence AI outputs, including how prompt design affects results, why AI produces certain types of errors, and how different AI systems have different capabilities and limitations.

Students can discuss current state and trajectory of AI development, including recent advances, ongoing limitations, and credible projections for near-term development. They can distinguish between demonstrated AI capabilities and speculation or hype.
Students understand AI applications relevant to their subjects and potential career paths. They can discuss how AI might change work and study in fields of interest to them.

strand2.title: Using AI Responsibly — Upper Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Upper Secondary, students use AI tools effectively and efficiently for appropriate academic and personal tasks. They demonstrate advanced prompt engineering skills, crafting detailed prompts that produce high-quality, targeted outputs.

Students exercise sophisticated judgment about when AI use is appropriate, beneficial, or inappropriate. They navigate academic integrity requirements confidently, understanding expectations for different contexts and assignment types.

Students can use AI to enhance their learning and productivity without undermining their own skill development. They understand the difference between using AI to learn more effectively and using AI to avoid learning.
Students document and cite AI use appropriately when required. They maintain their own voice and thinking in AI-assisted work.

strand3.title: Evaluating AI Outputs — Upper Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Upper Secondary, students apply rigorous evaluation standards to AI outputs, assessing accuracy, completeness, depth, bias, and suitability for purpose. They can identify subtle errors, limitations, and biases that require careful analysis to detect.

Students integrate information from AI with other sources, synthesising and evaluating across multiple inputs. They can assess the credibility and quality of AI-generated content relative to expert sources.
Students can evaluate AI applications and claims critically, distinguishing genuine utility from hype and identifying potential limitations or risks.

strand4.title: AI and Society — Upper Secondary
verbatim: By the end of Upper Secondary, students can analyse complex AI-related social issues with nuance and multiple perspectives. They understand economic, political, ethical, and social dimensions of AI development and deployment.

Students can discuss regulatory and governance challenges related to AI, including questions of accountability, transparency, and control. They understand issues of AI safety and alignment at a conceptual level.

Students can evaluate ethical frameworks for AI development and use, considering different values and priorities. They can articulate and defend their own views on AI-related issues with reasoned arguments.

Students understand their responsibilities as future professionals, citizens, and leaders in an AI-transformed society. They are prepared to engage constructively with AI developments throughout their lives.

AI literacy at Upper Secondary should be deeply integrated across the curriculum and also addressed explicitly in appropriate courses. Computing and technology courses can address technical aspects in depth. All subjects can incorporate AI literacy relevant to their discipline, including AI applications in the field, critical evaluation of AI-generated content, and ethical considerations.
Extended essays, research projects, and other major assessments should include clear expectations for AI use and require students to demonstrate their own thinking and analysis.
Students should have substantial opportunities for independent AI use with high expectations for responsibility, critical evaluation, and academic integrity.

Student AI competency should be assessed primarily through ongoing formative assessment embedded in regular instruction. Teachers observe student AI use during guided activities, assess critical evaluation through discussion and written reflection, and monitor responsible use through regular classroom practice.
Feedback should help students understand their current capabilities and how to improve.

Student AI competency may be assessed through performance tasks requiring AI use with critical reflection, written assessments of AI knowledge and understanding, projects demonstrating responsible and effective AI application, and oral presentations or discussions on AI-related topics.
Assessment should be age-appropriate and integrated with subject learning objectives where possible.

Student progress in AI literacy should be reported to parents through regular reporting mechanisms. Reports should indicate progress against age-appropriate expectations and identify areas for continued development.

_indexing.competencyBlockMatrix: 3 age bands × 4 strands = 12 strand blocks + 3 age-band summaries + 3 integration guidance blocks + 1 overview + 1 strands overview + 1 assessment = 21 referenceable blocks
